natural_occurrence.description: Beta-cyclodextrin does not occur naturally in significant amounts. It is produced by enzymatic modification of starch, a natural polysaccharide. The cyclodextrin structure consists of 7 glucose units arranged in a ring, creating a molecular cage.

manufacturing.description: Produced by enzymatic degradation of starch using cyclodextrin glycosyltransferase (CGTase) enzyme, typically from Bacillus bacteria. The enzyme converts linear starch chains into cyclic structures. Beta-cyclodextrin contains 7 glucose units in a ring, forming a truncated cone shape with a hydrophobic (water-repelling) cavity and hydrophilic (water-loving) exterior. This unique structure allows it to encapsulate guest molecules.

other_uses.cosmetics: Stabilizer and odor absorber in cosmetics and personal care products.
other_uses.industrial: Odor control (Febreze uses beta-cyclodextrin to trap odor molecules), textile treatment, pharmaceutical excipient.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Drug delivery system, solubility enhancer, stabilizer for sensitive active ingredients.
other_uses.household: Febreze and other odor eliminators (traps odor molecules in molecular cage), air fresheners.

eu.conditions: Approved for use in specified food categories; not widely used in foods compared to pharmaceutical and industrial applications.

usa.notes: More commonly used in pharmaceuticals and consumer products than direct food use

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: Not specified (considered safe at levels used)

food_categories_eu.category_name: Other confectionery including breath freshening microsweets

commonly_found_in: Febreze and odor-eliminating sprays (traps odor molecules)
Some processed foods for flavor stability
fun_facts: Beta-cyclodextrin is the active ingredient in Febreze - it works by trapping stinky odor molecules inside its molecular cage, not by masking them with perfume
The molecule has a doughnut or truncated cone shape with a hollow center - like a molecular trap for other molecules
Because it's made from starch (which your body can digest), cyclodextrin passes through your digestive system unchanged - your enzymes can't break the unusual ring structure
In pharmaceuticals, cyclodextrins can make poorly soluble drugs dissolve better by encapsulating them - it's like molecular packaging
The cavity size of beta-cyclodextrin (7 glucose units) is just right for many flavor and odor molecules - alpha (6 units) is too small, gamma (8 units) is too large
